questions.question: Which phase is most likely to be a bottleneck when running on a heavily utilized quantum backend?
questions.explanation: On heavily utilized backends, queue wait time often becomes the dominant bottleneck as jobs wait for available resources, potentially taking minutes to hours.

questions.question: What is the recommended approach to address transpilation bottlenecks?

options.text: Cache transpiled circuits and use lower optimization levels

questions.explanation: Caching transpiled circuits avoids repeated compilation, and using lower optimization levels (e.g., level 1 instead of 3) reduces transpilation time at the cost of some circuit quality.

questions.question: When profiling shows execution is the bottleneck for a simulator backend, what should you investigate?

options.text: Circuit depth, qubit count, and shot count

questions.explanation: For simulators, execution time depends on circuit depth, qubit count (exponential state vector size), and shot count. These are the key factors to investigate.

questions.question: What indicates a high-variability bottleneck in a quantum workflow?
questions.explanation: A high coefficient of variation (standard deviation divided by mean) indicates unpredictable performance, suggesting external factors like queue times or system load affecting results.

questions.question: Which metric is most useful for comparing optimization levels in transpilation?

options.text: Ratio of depth reduction to compilation time

questions.explanation: The ratio of depth reduction to compilation time captures the trade-off between quality (depth reduction) and cost (time), allowing meaningful comparison between optimization levels.

questions.question: Bottlenecks in quantum workflows can shift after optimization - a phase that was fast may become the new bottleneck.
questions.explanation: True. When you optimize one bottleneck (e.g., transpilation), another phase (e.g., execution) may become the new dominant bottleneck. Continuous profiling is essential.

questions.question: What is the best strategy for handling result processing bottlenecks with large shot counts?
questions.explanation: Streaming or incremental processing allows handling large result sets without loading everything into memory at once, reducing both memory pressure and processing bottlenecks.

questions.question: Which are effective methods for detecting performance anomalies in real-time?

options.text: Compare current metrics to rolling average
Monitor standard deviation over a window

questions.explanation: Rolling averages, standard deviation monitoring, and threshold alerts are effective real-time detection methods. Full regression tests are too slow for real-time monitoring.
